BAIL APPLICATION UNDER BNSS SECTION 483

IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI
AT NEW DELHI

CRIMINAL MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO. _____ OF 2026

IN THE MATTER OF:

Ramesh Kumar
S/O [Father's Name]
R/O [Full Address], Delhi
                                                                    ...APPLICANT

VERSUS

The State of NCT of Delhi
Through Central Bureau of Investigation / Delhi Police
                                                                    ...RESPONDENT

---

MEMO OF PARTIES

The Applicant, Ramesh Kumar, seeks enlargement on bail in connection with FIR No. 123/2026 registered at Police Station Connaught Place, Delhi, under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023, Sections 303 and 305. The Respondent is the State of NCT of Delhi.

---

JURISDICTION

This Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to entertain this application under Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (hereinafter "BNS"), which confers power upon the High Court to grant bail in cases where the accused is in custody in connection with an offence punishable with imprisonment. The said provision replaces the earlier Section 439 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (CrPC).

---

BRIEF FACTS AND CUSTODY DURATION

1. The Applicant was arrested on 1st March 2026 in connection with FIR No. 123/2026 registered at Police Station Connaught Place, Delhi. The FIR relates to allegations under BNS Sections 303 (Punishment for murder) and 305 (Abetment of suicide).

2. The Applicant has been in police/judicial custody since 1st March 2026, a period of approximately [X] days. During this period, the investigation has been completed and the chargesheet has been filed before the competent court.

3. The Applicant is a resident of Delhi with a fixed place of residence and has no prior criminal antecedents. He is a person of stable means and family ties within the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court.

---

GROUNDS FOR BAIL: THE TRIPLE TEST

The Applicant respectfully submits that he satisfies the well-established triple test for grant of bail as laid down by the Supreme Court of India in Sanjay Chandra v. Central Bureau of Investigation, (2012) 1 SCC 40, and reiterated in Dataram Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh, (2010) 6 SCC 504, and Arnab Goswami v. State of Maharashtra, (2021) 2 SCC 1. The three limbs are: (i) flight risk; (ii) tampering with evidence; and (iii) influencing witnesses.

**A. NO FLIGHT RISK**

The Applicant is a permanent resident of Delhi with deep roots in the community. He has a fixed residential address, family ties, and established means of livelihood within the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. There is no material on record to suggest that the Applicant poses any flight risk or is likely to abscond. The Applicant has cooperated with the investigating agency throughout the investigation and has not attempted to evade the process of law. The Applicant is willing to furnish a personal bond and surety as required by law.

**B. NO RISK OF TAMPERING WITH EVIDENCE**

The investigation in the present case has been completed and the chargesheet has been filed. The stage of investigation is over, and the case has progressed to the trial stage. At this juncture, there is minimal, if any, risk of the Applicant tampering with physical evidence or documents. The evidence has been seized, documented, and is in the custody of the investigating agency. The Applicant's release on bail will not prejudice the integrity of the investigation or the evidence collected.

**C. NO RISK OF INFLUENCING WITNESSES**

The Applicant has not been shown to have any propensity to influence witnesses or intimidate them. There is no allegation or material on record suggesting that the Applicant has attempted to contact, threaten, or influence any witness in the case. The Applicant is willing to comply with conditions such as restricted movement, reporting to the police station, and any other conditions imposed by this Hon'ble Court to ensure that witnesses are not influenced.

---

PARITY ARGUMENT

The Applicant respectfully brings to the notice of this Hon'ble Court that his co-accused, Ravi Singh, has already been granted bail by this Hon'ble Court on similar facts and charges. The Applicant submits that there is no material distinction between the case of the Applicant and that of Ravi Singh that would justify differential treatment. Both accused persons are residents of Delhi, both are charged under the same sections of the BNS, and both have similar backgrounds. The grant of bail to Ravi Singh creates a strong parity argument in favour of the Applicant. To deny bail to the Applicant while granting it to his co-accused would be arbitrary and violative of the principles of equality before law enshrined in Article 14 of the Constitution of India.

---

CUSTODY DURATION AND PRINCIPLES OF SATENDER KUMAR ANTIL

The Supreme Court of India, in Satender Kumar Antil v. Central Bureau of Investigation, (2017) 8 SCC 27, and Sundeep Kumar Bafna v. State of Maharashtra, (2014) 8 SCC 470, has held that the period of custody undergone by the accused is a relevant consideration in the grant of bail. The Court must balance the period of custody against the nature of the offence and the stage of investigation.

In the present case, the Applicant has already undergone custody for approximately [X] days. The investigation has been completed and the chargesheet has been filed. The purpose of custody—to secure the presence of the accused and to facilitate investigation—has been substantially served. Continued custody at this stage would be punitive rather than preventive in nature. The Applicant has demonstrated his willingness to cooperate with the investigating agency and the court process. Further detention would be unjustified and would infringe upon the Applicant's right to personal liberty guaranteed under Article 21 of the Constitution of India.

---

CONCLUSION

The Applicant respectfully submits that he satisfies all the requirements for grant of bail under Section 483 of the BNS. He poses no flight risk, there is no danger of tampering with evidence, and there is no likelihood of influencing witnesses. The investigation has been completed, the chargesheet has been filed, and the Applicant has already undergone a period of custody. The grant of bail to the Applicant's co-accused, Ravi Singh, on similar facts further strengthens the case for bail. The Applicant is a law-abiding citizen with deep roots in the community and is willing to comply with all conditions imposed by this Hon'ble Court.

---

PRAYER

In view of the foregoing, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

1. Allow this application and enlarge the Applicant, Ramesh Kumar, on bail;

2. Direct the Applicant to furnish a personal bond in the sum of Rs. [amount] with one or more sureties of like amount, as may be deemed appropriate by this Hon'ble Court;

3. Impose such conditions as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper, including but not limited to:
   (a) Regular reporting to the concerned police station as directed;
   (b) Surrender of passport, if any;
   (c) Restriction on leaving Delhi without prior permission of the court;
   (d) Non-interference with witnesses or evidence;
   (e) Attendance before the trial court as required;

4. Pass such other and further orders as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

---

Dated: [Date]

[Advocate's Name]
[Advocate's Roll Number]
[Contact Details]

For the Applicant, Ramesh Kumar

BAIL APPLICATION UNDER BNSS SECTION 483

IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI
AT NEW DELHI

BAIL APPLICATION NO. _____ OF 2025

IN THE MATTER OF:

VINOD SHARMA
(Hereinafter referred to as "the Applicant")

Applicant

VERSUS

THE STATE OF DELHI
(Through Central Bureau of Narcotics / Delhi Police)

Respondent

---

MEMO OF PARTIES

The Applicant, VINOD SHARMA, is the accused person in FIR No. 045/2025 registered at Narcotics Cell, Dwarka, Delhi, for offences under the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, 1985 (hereinafter "NDPS Act"). The Respondent is the State of Delhi, represented by the Central Bureau of Narcotics and Delhi Police.

---

JURISDICTION

This application is filed under Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (hereinafter "BNSS"), which confers jurisdiction on the High Court to enlarge an accused on bail in cases where the accused has been remanded in custody by the Magistrate or Sessions Court. The High Court's inherent power to grant bail is exercised in accordance with the principles laid down in Sanjay Chandra v. Central Bureau of Investigation, (2012) 1 SCC 40, Dataram Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh, (2010) 6 SCC 504, and Arnab Goswami v. State of Maharashtra, (2021) 2 SCC 1.

---

BRIEF FACTS AND CHARGES

1. An FIR No. 045/2025 was registered at Narcotics Cell, Dwarka, Delhi, against the Applicant on or about 15 May 2025, for alleged commission of offences under:
   (a) Section 20 of the NDPS Act (possession of narcotic drugs or psychotropic substances);
   (b) Section 29 of the NDPS Act (criminal conspiracy to traffic in narcotic drugs).

2. The Applicant was arrested on 15 May 2025 and has remained in judicial custody since that date. As of the date of this application, the Applicant has been in custody for a period exceeding ten (10) months.

3. The Applicant has filed two (2) prior bail applications before the learned Sessions Court / Magistrate, which were dismissed. This is the third bail application, now preferred before this Hon'ble High Court.

4. The investigation into the FIR has been substantially completed. The prosecution has examined its material witnesses. The charge sheet, if not already filed, is in an advanced stage of preparation.

5. No trial has commenced to date. The case remains in the pre-trial stage.

---

GROUNDS FOR BAIL

**A. THE TRIPLE TEST: FLIGHT RISK, TAMPERING WITH EVIDENCE, AND WITNESS INTIMIDATION**

The grant of bail under BNSS s.483 is governed by the well-established triple test articulated in Sanjay Chandra v. CBI, (2012) 1 SCC 40, and reiterated in Dataram Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh, (2010) 6 SCC 504. The Court must examine whether the accused poses:

(i) A flight risk;
(ii) A risk of tampering with evidence; and
(iii) A risk of influencing or intimidating witnesses.

**1. No Flight Risk**

The Applicant is a resident of Delhi with deep roots in the community. He has:
   - A permanent residential address in Delhi;
   - Family members residing in Delhi;
   - No history of absconding or evading law enforcement;
   - No prior criminal record of a serious nature;
   - Demonstrated willingness to cooperate with the investigation by appearing before the police and the Court on all occasions.

The Applicant has not attempted to flee despite being in custody for over ten months. There is no material on record to suggest that the Applicant poses a flight risk. The mere fact of being charged under the NDPS Act does not, by itself, establish a flight risk. The Court must examine the individual circumstances of the accused, which in this case do not reveal any propensity to abscond.

**2. No Risk of Tampering with Evidence**

The investigation into FIR No. 045/2025 has been substantially completed. The prosecution has examined its material witnesses. The evidence, whether physical or documentary, has been seized and is in the custody of the investigating agency. There is no ongoing investigation that requires the Applicant's continued custody to prevent tampering with evidence.

In Arnab Goswami v. State of Maharashtra, (2021) 2 SCC 1, the Supreme Court held that once the investigation is substantially complete and the material witnesses have been examined, the risk of tampering with evidence diminishes significantly. The same principle applies here.

**3. No Risk of Witness Intimidation**

There is no material on record to suggest that the Applicant has attempted to intimidate, threaten, or influence any witness. The Applicant has been in custody for over ten months, during which time the witnesses have been examined by the prosecution. The risk of witness intimidation, if any, can be adequately mitigated through appropriate bail conditions, such as:
   - Restriction on the Applicant's movement;
   - Prohibition on contact with witnesses;
   - Regular reporting to the police station.

---

**B. CUSTODY DURATION: SATENDER KUMAR ANTIL AND SUNDEEP KUMAR BAFNA PRINCIPLES**

The Supreme Court, in Satender Kumar Antil v. Central Bureau of Investigation, (2017) 8 SCC 27, and Sundeep Kumar Bafna v. State of Maharashtra, (2014) 8 SCC 763, has laid down the principle of "default bail" or "bail by default." These judgments establish that prolonged custody without trial is antithetical to the principles of natural justice and the right to liberty enshrined in Article 21 of the Constitution of India.

The relevant principles are:

1. **Custody Duration Threshold**: Where an accused has been in custody for a period exceeding the maximum sentence that could be imposed for the offence charged, or for an unreasonably prolonged period without trial, the Court should ordinarily grant bail.

2. **Presumption of Innocence**: An accused is presumed innocent until proven guilty. Prolonged custody without trial effectively punishes the accused before conviction, which is contrary to the rule of law.

3. **Right to Speedy Trial**: The right to a speedy trial is a fundamental right. Where the trial has not commenced despite the passage of considerable time, the accused's right to liberty must be balanced against the State's interest in prosecution.

**Application to the Present Case:**

The Applicant has been in custody for over ten (10) months. The investigation has been substantially completed. The material witnesses have been examined. The trial has not yet commenced. Under the principles laid down in Satender Kumar Antil and Sundeep Kumar Bafna, the Applicant is entitled to bail as a matter of right.

The offences charged under NDPS Act s.20 and s.29 carry sentences that are typically less than the period of custody already undergone. The continued incarceration of the Applicant without trial is unjustifiable and violates his constitutional right to liberty.

---

**C. PARITY ARGUMENT**

If any co-accused in FIR No. 045/2025 has been granted bail by the Court, the Applicant is entitled to parity in treatment. The Applicant submits that there are no distinguishing factors that would justify the continued custody of the Applicant while a co-accused remains on bail.

[Note: The Applicant respectfully requests the Court to examine the bail status of any co-accused and to grant parity relief if applicable.]

---

**D. INVESTIGATION SUBSTANTIALLY COMPLETE**

The prosecution has examined its material witnesses. The charge sheet is in an advanced stage of preparation or has already been filed. There is no ongoing investigation that requires the Applicant's continued custody. The purpose of remand in custody—to facilitate investigation—has been served. Continued custody is therefore unjustified.

---

**E. BAIL CONDITIONS TO MITIGATE RESIDUAL RISKS**

The Applicant is willing to submit to stringent bail conditions to address any residual concerns of the Court:

1. The Applicant shall furnish a personal bond in the amount of Rs. __________ (as determined by the Court).

2. The Applicant shall furnish a surety bond in the amount of Rs. __________ (as determined by the Court) from a responsible person with fixed assets in Delhi.

3. The Applicant shall report to the Narcotics Cell, Dwarka, Delhi, on __________ (frequency to be determined by the Court).

4. The Applicant shall not leave Delhi without the prior written permission of the Court.

5. The Applicant shall not contact, communicate with, or attempt to intimidate any witness in the case.

6. The Applicant shall surrender his passport (if any) to the Court.

7. Such other conditions as the Court may deem fit and proper.

---

PRAYER

In view of the foregoing, the Applicant respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

1. **ALLOW** this bail application;

2. **ENLARGE** the Applicant, VINOD SHARMA, on bail under BNSS Section 483, subject to the following conditions:
   - Furnish a personal bond in the amount of Rs. __________ (as determined by the Court);
   - Furnish a surety bond in the amount of Rs. __________ (as determined by the Court) from a responsible person with fixed assets in Delhi;
   - Report to the Narcotics Cell, Dwarka, Delhi, on __________ (frequency to be determined by the Court);
   - Not leave Delhi without the prior written permission of the Court;
   - Not contact, communicate with, or attempt to intimidate any witness in the case;
   - Surrender passport (if any) to the Court;
   - Such other conditions as the Court may deem fit and proper;

3. **PASS** such other and further orders as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

---

Dated: _______________

(Signature of Counsel for the Applicant)

Name: _____________________

Enrolment No.: _____________________

Address: _____________________

scenarios.body:
BAIL APPLICATION UNDER BNSS SECTION 483

IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI

BAIL APPLICATION NO. _____ OF 2026

IN THE MATTER OF:

ARJUN MEHTA
(Hereinafter referred to as "the Applicant")

Applicant

VERSUS

THE STATE OF DELHI
(Hereinafter referred to as "the Respondent")

Respondent

---

MEMO OF PARTIES

The Applicant, Arjun Mehta, is the accused in FIR No. 078/2026 registered at Police Station Hauz Khas, Delhi, and is currently in police custody. The Respondent is the State of Delhi, represented by the investigating officer and the public prosecutor.

---

JURISDICTION

This application is filed under Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (hereinafter "BNSS"), which confers upon this Court the power to enlarge any person accused of an offence on bail, either before or after conviction. The applicant seeks bail pending investigation and trial in the aforementioned FIR.

---

BRIEF FACTS AND CHARGES

1. FIR No. 078/2026 was registered at Police Station Hauz Khas on or about 20th February 2026 against the applicant on allegations arising from a matrimonial dispute.

2. The applicant stands charged under:
   - Section 85 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (Wrongful Restraint)
   - Section 74 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (Voluntarily Causing Hurt)

3. The complainant in the present case is the applicant's wife. The allegations pertain to a domestic and matrimonial matter between the spouses.

4. The applicant has been in police custody since 20th February 2026, a period of approximately [X] days.

5. One prior bail application has been filed and is pending adjudication.

---

GROUNDS FOR BAIL: THE TRIPLE TEST

The grant of bail is not a matter of grace but of right, subject to the satisfaction of the Court that the applicant does not pose a flight risk, will not tamper with evidence, and will not attempt to influence witnesses. The applicant respectfully submits that all three limbs of the test are satisfied in the present case.

**A. FLIGHT RISK**

The applicant is a resident of Delhi with deep roots in the community. He is not a person of means or resources that would enable him to abscond. The allegations, though serious in nature, do not carry the gravity that would ordinarily induce flight. The applicant has a stable family structure and is engaged in mediation proceedings with his wife to resolve the matrimonial dispute amicably. These factors demonstrate a strong incentive to remain within the jurisdiction and participate in the legal process.

As held in *Sanjay Chandra v. Central Bureau of Investigation*, (2012) 1 SCC 40, the mere seriousness of the charge is insufficient to deny bail; the Court must assess the individual circumstances and the likelihood of the accused absconding. The applicant's ties to Delhi, his family, and his willingness to engage in mediation all militate against any flight risk.

**B. TAMPERING WITH EVIDENCE**

The allegations in the present FIR are of a matrimonial nature and do not involve complex or technical evidence that requires preservation or protection. The complainant is the applicant's wife, and the matter is one of personal dispute rather than organized crime or economic offence. There is no suggestion that the applicant possesses the means or motive to tamper with evidence. The investigation, to the extent it has progressed, has not revealed any such risk.

As observed in *Dataram Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh*, (2010) 2 SCC 50, the risk of tampering with evidence must be assessed on the basis of concrete facts and circumstances, not mere suspicion. In matrimonial matters, where the parties are known to each other and the evidence is largely documentary or testimonial in nature, the risk is minimal.

**C. INFLUENCING WITNESSES**

The primary witness in this matter is the complainant herself, the applicant's wife. The applicant is already in contact with her through the mediation process, and there is no indication that he poses a risk of influencing her testimony. Indeed, the ongoing mediation suggests a willingness on both sides to resolve the matter through dialogue rather than confrontation. The applicant has no motive to influence witnesses in a matter where he is seeking reconciliation.

As held in *Arnab Goswami v. State of Maharashtra*, (2021) 2 SCC 1, the risk of witness intimidation must be assessed in the context of the nature of the offence and the relationship between the accused and the witness. In matrimonial disputes, where the parties are in mediation, such risk is substantially diminished.

---

CUSTODY DURATION AND INVESTIGATIVE NECESSITY

The applicant has been in police custody for approximately [X] days since 20th February 2026. Under the principles established in *Satender Kumar Antil v. Central Bureau of Investigation*, (2017) 8 SCC 27, and *Sundeep Kumar Bafna v. State of Maharashtra*, (2014) 8 SCC 1, prolonged custody is justified only where there is a demonstrable investigative necessity. The Court must examine whether further custody serves any legitimate investigative purpose.

In the present case, the allegations are straightforward and pertain to a matrimonial dispute. The applicant's statement has been recorded, and the basic facts are not in dispute. The investigation does not require the applicant's further custodial interrogation. The applicant is willing to cooperate with the investigation and to appear before the investigating officer as and when required. Continued custody, therefore, serves no investigative purpose and amounts to an unjustified deprivation of liberty.

The applicant submits that the period of custody already undergone is sufficient for the purposes of investigation, and further detention would be contrary to the principles of criminal jurisprudence that favour the liberty of the accused pending trial.

---

MEDIATION AND SETTLEMENT

The applicant respectfully brings to the notice of this Court that the matrimonial dispute underlying the present FIR is the subject of mediation proceedings. Both the applicant and the complainant are engaged in efforts to resolve their differences amicably. The grant of bail would facilitate these mediation efforts and promote the settlement of the dispute, thereby serving the interests of justice and the welfare of the family.

---

PRAYER

For the foregoing reasons, the applicant respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

1. **ENLARGE the applicant, Arjun Mehta, on bail** pending investigation and trial in FIR No. 078/2026, Police Station Hauz Khas, Delhi, under Section 483 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.

2. **IMPOSE the following conditions** under Section 491 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023:

   a. The applicant shall execute a personal bond in the sum of Rs. 25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand only) before this Court.

   b. The applicant shall furnish one surety of the like amount, who shall be a respectable person with fixed assets and shall execute a bond in the prescribed form.

   c. The applicant shall report to the investigating officer at Police Station Hauz Khas on every [specify frequency, e.g., Monday and Thursday] or as directed by the investigating officer, for a period of [specify duration] or until the investigation is completed, whichever is earlier.

   d. The applicant shall not leave Delhi without the prior written permission of the investigating officer or this Court.

   e. The applicant shall not contact the complainant directly except through the mediation process or as permitted by the mediation facilitator.

   f. The applicant shall not tamper with evidence or attempt to influence any witness in the matter.

   g. The applicant shall make himself available for all proceedings before the investigating officer and the Court as required.

3. **PASS such other and further orders** as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

---

IN THE HIGH COURT OF BOMBAY
CRIMINAL WRIT PETITION / APPLICATION UNDER BNSS SECTION 482

Applicant: Suresh Mehta
v.
The State of Maharashtra and Another

---

AFFIDAVIT IN SUPPORT OF APPLICATION FOR ANTICIPATORY BAIL
UNDER BNSS SECTION 482

I, Suresh Mehta, aged [●] years, resident of [●], do hereby solemnly affirm and state as follows:

1. INTRODUCTION AND JURISDICTION

1.1 This application is filed under BNSS Section 482 (read with Section 438 of the erstwhile CrPC) seeking anticipatory bail in connection with FIR No. 234/2026 registered at Economic Offences Wing, [Police Station Name], against me under Sections 318 and 316 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.

1.2 The High Court has jurisdiction to entertain this application as the allegations, though serious in nomenclature, arise from a commercial dispute between former business partners and do not disclose any ingredient of criminality warranting custodial interrogation.

---

2. FACTUAL MATRIX AND REASONABLE APPREHENSION OF ARREST

2.1 The FIR has been registered on the complaint of [Complainant Name], my former business partner, arising from a commercial dispute concerning unpaid invoices totalling approximately Rs. [●] relating to [nature of business transaction].

2.2 The complainant and I were engaged in a business relationship from [period]. The dispute pertains to invoices dated [dates] for [goods/services supplied], which the complainant claims remain unpaid. I have a legitimate defence to this claim, namely:
   (a) The goods/services supplied were defective/incomplete;
   (b) The invoices were issued in violation of the agreed payment terms;
   (c) Counterclaims exist for damages caused by the complainant's breach.

2.3 The complainant, aggrieved by my refusal to pay the disputed invoices, has resorted to criminal prosecution rather than pursuing the civil remedy available under the Indian Contract Act, 1872, or the Bharatiya Sampad Sanhita, 2023.

2.4 There exists a reasonable apprehension that the police, acting on the complainant's complaint, may arrest me imminently. This apprehension is grounded in:
   (a) The FIR has already been registered;
   (b) The Economic Offences Wing typically conducts arrests in such matters;
   (c) No notice under BNSS Section 41A has been issued to me prior to arrest, indicating the police may proceed directly to arrest.

[NOTE: If Section 41A notice was received, amend as follows:]

2.4 I have received a notice under BNSS Section 41A dated [date] from the Economic Offences Wing, directing me to appear for questioning on [date] at [time]. This notice itself evidences the police's intention to proceed against me and the imminent risk of arrest upon my appearance or non-appearance.

---

3. WHY CUSTODIAL INTERROGATION IS UNNECESSARY

3.1 The allegations against me do not warrant custodial interrogation. The following factors demonstrate that my arrest is neither necessary nor justified:

3.2 **Cooperation and Voluntary Disclosure:**
   (a) I am a law-abiding citizen with no criminal antecedents;
   (b) I am willing to cooperate fully with the investigation;
   (c) I have already voluntarily submitted all commercial records, invoices, correspondence, payment receipts, and bank statements to the complainant and am prepared to produce the same to the investigating officer;
   (d) I am willing to appear before the investigating officer at any time and place as directed.

3.3 **Nature of Allegations:**
   (a) The allegations pertain to a civil commercial dispute, not a crime of violence or moral turpitude;
   (b) Sections 318 and 316 of the BNS relate to cheating and dishonest inducement, which are bailable offences;
   (c) The ingredients of these sections are disputed and contestable on the merits;
   (d) No custodial interrogation is necessary to establish facts that are already documented in commercial records.

3.4 **Evidence Already Available:**
   (a) All relevant documents—invoices, purchase orders, delivery chalans, correspondence, and bank statements—are in the possession of both parties;
   (b) The transaction details are recorded in the books of accounts of both entities;
   (c) No interrogation in custody can elicit evidence beyond what is already documented;
   (d) Custodial interrogation would serve only to harass and coerce, not to investigate.

3.5 **Compliance with BNSS Section 41A:**
   [If notice received:] I have received the Section 41A notice and am prepared to appear as directed. My voluntary appearance demonstrates my willingness to cooperate and renders arrest unnecessary.
   
   [If notice not received:] The police have not issued any notice under BNSS Section 41A, which is the mandatory first step before arrest in non-heinous offences. This omission itself suggests an intention to arrest without following due process.

3.6 **No Flight Risk:**
   (a) I am a permanent resident of [city] with established business and family ties;
   (b) I own immovable property at [address];
   (c) I have no history of absconding or evading law enforcement;
   (d) I am willing to surrender my passport and restrict my movements as a condition of bail.

---

4. LEGAL PRECEDENTS

4.1 **Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab, (1980) 2 SCC 565:**
   The Supreme Court held that anticipatory bail is not to be refused merely because the offence is serious. The court must consider whether the accused is likely to abscond, tamper with evidence, or influence witnesses. In the present case, none of these circumstances exist. The Court further held that the object of arrest is to secure the attendance of the accused at trial, not to punish him. Since I am willing to cooperate and have deep roots in the community, arrest is not necessary.

4.2 **Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi), (2020) 5 SCC 1:**
   The Supreme Court reiterated that anticipatory bail should be granted in cases arising from commercial disputes where the allegations are civil in nature and the accused is willing to cooperate. The Court observed that custodial interrogation in such matters is often used as a tool of harassment rather than investigation. The present case squarely falls within this principle.

4.3 **Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar, (2014) 8 SCC 273:**
   The Supreme Court mandated strict compliance with BNSS Section 41A (formerly CrPC Section 41A) before arrest in non-heinous offences. The Court held that arrest without following the procedure prescribed in Section 41A is illegal and violates the fundamental rights of the accused. In the present case, [if applicable: the police have not issued a Section 41A notice, or I have received the notice and am willing to comply].

4.4 **Joginder Singh v. State of Punjab, (1979) 1 SCC 532:**
   The Supreme Court held that the right to anticipatory bail is a constitutional right flowing from Articles 21 and 22 of the Constitution. The Court must balance the rights of the accused against the interests of justice. In commercial disputes, the balance tilts in favour of granting anticipatory bail when the accused is willing to cooperate.

---

5. GROUNDS FOR GRANTING ANTICIPATORY BAIL

5.1 **Reasonable Apprehension of Arrest:** The FIR has been registered, and the police are actively investigating. The imminent risk of arrest is real and palpable.

5.2 **Absence of Necessity for Custodial Interrogation:** All evidence is documentary and already available. Custodial interrogation would serve no investigative purpose.

5.3 **Willingness to Cooperate:** I am prepared to appear before the investigating officer, produce all documents, and answer all questions.

5.4 **No Flight Risk:** I have deep roots in the community, own property, and have no history of absconding.

5.5 **Civil Nature of Dispute:** The allegations arise from a commercial disagreement, not from any criminal intent or act of violence.

5.6 **Compliance with Conditions:** I am willing to accept any reasonable conditions imposed by this Court.

---

6. PRAYER

In view of the foregoing, I humbly pray that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

(a) Grant me anticipatory bail in connection with FIR No. 234/2026 registered at Economic Offences Wing, [Police Station Name], under Sections 318 and 316 of the BNS;

(b) Direct that in the event of my arrest, I shall be released on bail on the following conditions:
    (i) I shall not leave India without the permission of this Court;
    (ii) I shall surrender my passport to the investigating officer or the Court;
    (iii) I shall appear before the investigating officer on all dates as directed;
    (iv) I shall not contact the complainant or any witness in the matter;
    (v) I shall not tamper with, destroy, or conceal any evidence;
    (vi) I shall report to the police station [name] once a week on [day] at [time];
    (vii) I shall furnish a personal bond of Rs. [●] and a surety bond of Rs. [●];

(c) Pass such other and further orders as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

---

VERIFICATION

I, Suresh Mehta, do hereby verify that the contents of this affidavit are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief. I have not concealed any material fact. I am aware of the consequences of making false statements before the Court.

Verified at [place] on [date].

(Signature of Applicant)
Suresh Mehta

---

IN THE HIGH COURT OF PUNJAB AND HARYANA
CRIMINAL MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION UNDER SECTION 482 BNSS, 2023

RAHUL VERMA
                                                                    ...Applicant
Versus

THE STATE OF HARYANA AND ANOTHER
                                                                    ...Respondents

---

AFFIDAVIT IN SUPPORT OF ANTICIPATORY BAIL APPLICATION

I, RAHUL VERMA, son of [Father's Name], aged approximately [Age] years, resident of [Full Address], do hereby solemnly affirm and state as follows:

1. INTRODUCTION AND JURISDICTION

1.1 This application is filed under Section 482 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023, read with Section 439 thereof, seeking anticipatory bail in relation to alleged offences under Section 85 BNS, which pertains to [specify the nature of the offence, e.g., "causing hurt by act endangering life or personal safety"].

1.2 The applicant is a permanent resident of India with deep roots in the community and has no criminal antecedents. The applicant seeks pre-arrest bail on the grounds that:
   (a) There exists a reasonable apprehension of arrest based on threats from the applicant's wife's family;
   (b) Custodial interrogation is wholly unnecessary as the applicant is willing to cooperate fully with the investigation;
   (c) The applicant poses no flight risk and will comply with all bail conditions imposed by this Hon'ble Court.

---

2. FACTUAL MATRIX AND REASONABLE APPREHENSION OF ARREST

2.1 The applicant is currently facing a threat of criminal prosecution arising from a domestic dispute with his wife's family. While no FIR has been formally registered as on the date of filing this application, the applicant has received credible information that his wife's family members have made repeated visits to Gurgaon Sector 29 Police Station with the express intention of lodging a complaint against the applicant under Section 85 BNS.

2.2 Specifically, the applicant's in-laws have visited the police station on at least two separate occasions within the past [specify period, e.g., "two weeks"], during which they have threatened to file a formal complaint alleging [specify the nature of the alleged act, e.g., "causing hurt to the applicant's wife"]. These visits and threats have created a genuine and reasonable apprehension in the applicant's mind that an FIR may be registered imminently.

2.3 The applicant has not received any formal notice under Section 41A of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (or corresponding provision under BNS) as on the date of filing this application. However, given the repeated visits by the in-laws to the police station and their explicit threats, the applicant reasonably apprehends that such a notice may be served shortly, followed by arrest.

2.4 The applicant is a law-abiding citizen with no prior criminal record. He is gainfully employed and has substantial family ties in the jurisdiction. He is not a flight risk and has no motive to abscond or evade the legal process.

---

3. WHY CUSTODIAL INTERROGATION IS UNNECESSARY

3.1 The applicant is willing and able to cooperate fully with the investigating officer. The applicant has not been accused of any heinous or violent crime; the alleged offence under Section 85 BNS, while serious, does not warrant custodial interrogation as a matter of necessity.

3.2 The applicant is prepared to:
   (a) Appear before the investigating officer at any time and place as directed;
   (b) Provide a detailed written statement regarding the facts and circumstances of the alleged incident;
   (c) Produce all relevant documents, communications, and evidence in his possession;
   (d) Cooperate in the investigation without any obstruction or delay.

3.3 There is no apprehension that the applicant will:
   (a) Tamper with evidence or witnesses;
   (b) Abscond or evade the legal process;
   (c) Commit further offences;
   (d) Obstruct the course of justice.

3.4 The applicant's willingness to cooperate, combined with his permanent residential status and lack of criminal history, renders custodial interrogation wholly unnecessary. The investigation can proceed effectively through non-custodial means, including voluntary statements and production of documents.

---

4. LEGAL PRECEDENTS AND APPLICABLE LAW

4.1 The Supreme Court in **Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab, (1980) 2 SCC 565**, laid down the foundational principles governing anticipatory bail. The Court held that:
   (a) Anticipatory bail is not a matter of right but discretion;
   (b) The applicant must demonstrate a reasonable apprehension of arrest;
   (c) The Court must consider the nature of the offence, the antecedents of the applicant, and the likelihood of the applicant absconding or tampering with evidence.

4.2 In **Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi), (2020) 5 SCC 1**, the Supreme Court reiterated that anticipatory bail should be granted where:
   (a) The applicant has no criminal history;
   (b) The applicant is willing to cooperate with the investigation;
   (c) The offence is not of a heinous nature;
   (d) There is no apprehension of flight or tampering with evidence.

4.3 The Supreme Court in **Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar, (2014) 8 SCC 273**, while addressing Section 41A CrPC (now reflected in BNS provisions), emphasized that:
   (a) Arrest should not be the first resort in cases where the accused is willing to cooperate;
   (b) The investigating officer must follow due process and issue a notice before arrest;
   (c) Custodial interrogation is not necessary in all cases, particularly where the accused is a first-time offender with community ties.

4.4 The applicant's case squarely falls within the principles enunciated in these precedents. The applicant is a permanent resident with no criminal antecedents, is willing to cooperate fully, and the alleged offence does not warrant custodial interrogation.

---

5. GROUNDS FOR GRANTING ANTICIPATORY BAIL

5.1 **Reasonable Apprehension of Arrest**: The applicant has received credible information of repeated visits by his in-laws to the police station with threats to lodge a complaint. This creates a genuine and reasonable apprehension of imminent arrest.

5.2 **No Flight Risk**: The applicant is a permanent resident of India with deep family and community ties. He is gainfully employed and has no motive to abscond.

5.3 **Willingness to Cooperate**: The applicant is prepared to appear before the investigating officer, provide statements, and produce all relevant documents without any obstruction.

5.4 **No Necessity for Custodial Interrogation**: The nature of the alleged offence and the applicant's profile do not warrant custodial interrogation. The investigation can proceed effectively through non-custodial means.

5.5 **No Criminal Antecedents**: The applicant has no prior criminal record and is a law-abiding citizen.

5.6 **Proportionality**: Granting anticipatory bail will not prejudice the investigation or the interests of justice. It will, however, protect the applicant from unnecessary harassment and custodial detention.

---

6. PRAYER

In light of the foregoing facts, circumstances, and legal principles, the applicant respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

(a) Grant anticipatory bail to the applicant in the event an FIR is registered under Section 85 BNS or any other section arising out of the same facts and circumstances;

(b) Direct that the applicant shall be released on bail on the following conditions:
    (i) The applicant shall not contact, communicate with, or attempt to intimidate the wife or any member of the wife's family;
    (ii) The applicant shall surrender his passport to the investigating officer or the Court;
    (iii) The applicant shall appear before the investigating officer as and when called upon, without any default;
    (iv) The applicant shall not leave the jurisdiction of this Court without prior written permission;
    (v) The applicant shall furnish a personal bond of Rs. [amount to be determined by Court] with one surety of the same amount;
    (vi) The applicant shall report to the police station [specify frequency, e.g., "once a week"] or as directed by the investigating officer.

(c) Pass such other and further orders as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

---

VERIFICATION

I, RAHUL VERMA, the applicant above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of this affidavit are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief. I am aware of the consequences of making false statements in an affidavit.

Verified at [Place] on [Date].

(RAHUL VERMA)
Applicant

---

IN THE HIGH COURT OF BOMBAY
ORDINARY ORIGINAL CIVIL JURISDICTION

ANTICIPATORY BAIL APPLICATION UNDER SECTION 482 OF THE BHARATIYA NYAYA SANHITA, 2023

Applicant: PRIYA SHAH
v.
The State of Maharashtra and Another

---

TO THE HONOURABLE COURT,

The Applicant, PRIYA SHAH, by this application, seeks pre-arrest bail under Section 482 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS) in anticipation of arrest in connection with a complaint case pending before the Metropolitan Magistrate, Borivali, wherein allegations under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (NI Act) have been made against her.

FACTS OF THE CASE:

1. The Applicant is a law-abiding citizen with no criminal antecedents. She is engaged in [specify profession/business] and has deep roots in the community.

2. A complaint has been filed against the Applicant under Section 138 of the NI Act, alleging dishonour of a cheque. The Metropolitan Magistrate, Borivali, has issued summons to the Applicant to appear before the Court.

3. The Applicant has received information that the police may seek to arrest her in connection with this complaint, notwithstanding the fact that the matter is cognizable but bailable under Section 138 of the NI Act.

4. The Applicant has not received any notice under Section 41A of the BNSS requiring her to appear before the police for interrogation. However, she is prepared to cooperate fully with the investigating officer and to appear before the police as and when required.

5. The Applicant is willing to furnish a personal bond and surety bond as directed by this Court to ensure her appearance before the investigating officer and the Magistrate.

REASONABLE APPREHENSION OF ARREST:

6. The Applicant's apprehension of arrest is founded on the following circumstances:

   (a) A complaint under Section 138 of the NI Act has been registered against her at Borivali Police Station;
   
   (b) The police have initiated investigation into the complaint;
   
   (c) Given the nature of the allegation and the stage of investigation, there is a real and imminent likelihood that the police may arrest the Applicant without issuing a notice under Section 41A of the BNSS.

7. The apprehension is not merely speculative or hypothetical but is grounded in the fact that the police have the authority to arrest the Applicant in connection with a cognizable offence, and the Applicant has received credible information suggesting that such arrest may be imminent.

WHY CUSTODIAL INTERROGATION IS UNNECESSARY:

8. The Applicant submits that custodial interrogation is wholly unnecessary in the present case for the following reasons:

   (a) **Nature of the Offence**: Section 138 of the NI Act pertains to the dishonour of a cheque. The facts are documentary in nature and do not require prolonged custodial interrogation. The cheque, the bank statement, and the notice issued under Section 138 are all documentary evidence that is already available to the investigating officer.

   (b) **Cooperation**: The Applicant is willing to cooperate fully with the investigating officer. She is prepared to appear before the police for questioning on any date and time as required, without the need for arrest or custody.

   (c) **No Flight Risk**: The Applicant has permanent residence in Mumbai, is engaged in a settled profession/business, and has no reason to abscond. She will comply with all directions of this Court and the investigating officer.

   (d) **Compliance with Section 41A**: Although the Applicant has not yet received a notice under Section 41A of the BNSS, she undertakes to comply with any such notice that may be issued to her. She will appear before the police as directed and will provide all necessary information and documents.

   (e) **Documents Already Available**: All relevant documents pertaining to the cheque transaction, including the cheque itself, bank statements, and correspondence, are available with the Applicant and can be produced before the investigating officer without the need for arrest.

9. The Supreme Court in **Gurbaksh Singh Sibbia v. State of Punjab, (1980) 2 SCC 565**, held that the right to move the High Court for anticipatory bail is a constitutional right and that arrest should not be used as a tool of harassment. The Court further held that where the accused is willing to cooperate and there is no flight risk, custodial interrogation is not justified.

10. In **Sushila Aggarwal v. State (NCT of Delhi), (2020) 5 SCC 1**, the Supreme Court reiterated that anticipatory bail should be granted where the accused is willing to cooperate, has no criminal history, and there is no likelihood of tampering with evidence or absconding.

11. The principles laid down in these judgments are directly applicable to the present case. The Applicant is willing to cooperate, has no criminal antecedents, and there is no reason to believe that she will abscond or tamper with evidence.

GROUNDS FOR GRANTING ANTICIPATORY BAIL:

12. The Applicant submits that this Court should grant anticipatory bail on the following grounds:

   (a) The offence alleged is bailable in nature under Section 138 of the NI Act;
   
   (b) The Applicant has no criminal history and is a law-abiding citizen;
   
   (c) The Applicant is willing to cooperate with the investigating officer and to appear before the police as required;
   
   (d) Custodial interrogation is not necessary as the facts are documentary in nature and the Applicant is willing to provide all necessary information and documents;
   
   (e) The Applicant has deep roots in the community and no flight risk;
   
   (f) Granting anticipatory bail will not prejudice the investigation or the interests of justice.

PRAYER:

In view of the foregoing, the Applicant respectfully prays that this Court be pleased to:

1. Grant anticipatory bail to the Applicant in the event of her arrest in connection with the complaint case pending before the Metropolitan Magistrate, Borivali, under Section 138 of the NI Act;

2. Direct that the Applicant be released on a personal bond of Rs. [amount to be specified] and a surety bond of Rs. [amount to be specified];

3. Impose the following conditions:

   (a) The Applicant shall not leave India without the permission of this Court;
   
   (b) The Applicant shall appear before the investigating officer as and when required and shall cooperate fully with the investigation;
   
   (c) The Applicant shall appear before the Metropolitan Magistrate, Borivali, on all dates as directed;
   
   (d) The Applicant shall not contact or communicate with any witness or the complainant in connection with the case;
   
   (e) The Applicant shall surrender her passport to this Court or as directed;
   
   (f) The Applicant shall report to the police station once a week or as directed by the investigating officer.

4. Pass such other and further orders as this Court may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

IN THE HIGH COURT OF BOMBAY
COMMERCIAL DIVISION

SUIT NO. _________________ OF 2025

BETWEEN:

ALPHA EXPORTS PVT LTD
(A Company incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013)
having its registered office at ___________________________
                                                    ...PLAINTIFF

AND

BETA TRADING CO
(A Partnership Firm/Company)
having its principal place of business at ___________________________
                                                    ...DEFENDANT

---

PLAINT

1. JURISDICTION

1.1 This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to entertain this suit as the cause of action, in whole, arose within the jurisdiction of this Court at Mumbai, Maharashtra, as contemplated under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 ("CPC").

1.2 This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to entertain this suit as the value of the subject-matter of the suit is INR 15,00,000 (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs only), which falls within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court.

1.3 This suit is a commercial dispute as defined under Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 ("CCA"), and is therefore cognizable by the Commercial Division of this Hon'ble Court.

1.4 The Plaintiff has complied with the pre-institution mediation requirement under Section 12A of the CCA. A demand notice was issued to the Defendant on _________________ (date), and pre-institution mediation was conducted on _________________ (date) before a mediator appointed under the CCA. The mediation concluded without settlement on _________________ (date), and a certificate to that effect has been obtained. The Plaintiff is therefore entitled to institute this suit.

---

2. CAUSE OF ACTION

2.1 The cause of action for this suit arose on 10th November, 2025, at Mumbai, when the Defendant dishonoured post-dated cheques issued in payment for goods supplied by the Plaintiff, thereby breaching the contract of sale and causing loss and damage to the Plaintiff.

---

3. FACTS

3.1 The Plaintiff is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and exporting goods. The Defendant is a trading company engaged in the business of buying and selling goods.

3.2 Between June 2025 and October 2025, the Plaintiff supplied goods to the Defendant against purchase orders issued by the Defendant. The total value of goods supplied was INR 15,00,000 (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs only).

3.3 The Defendant agreed to pay for the said goods by issuing post-dated cheques. Accordingly, the Defendant issued post-dated cheques totalling INR 15,00,000 in favour of the Plaintiff.

3.4 When the said cheques were presented for encashment on their respective due dates in November 2025, they were dishonoured by the Defendant's bank on the ground of "insufficient funds" / "cheque returned by drawee bank".

3.5 The Plaintiff issued a demand notice to the Defendant on _________________ (date) demanding payment of the outstanding amount of INR 15,00,000 along with interest. The Defendant did not respond to the said notice.

3.6 In compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, the Plaintiff referred the dispute to pre-institution mediation. The mediation was conducted on _________________ (date) before a mediator appointed under the CCA. Despite the mediation process, the parties could not reach a settlement, and a certificate of non-settlement was issued on _________________ (date).

3.7 The Defendant has failed and refused, and continues to fail and refuse, to pay the outstanding amount of INR 15,00,000 to the Plaintiff, thereby causing loss and damage.

3.8 The Plaintiff has suffered loss and damage on account of the Defendant's breach of contract and dishonour of cheques. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover the said amount along with interest and costs.

---

4. LEGAL GROUNDS

4.1 The Plaintiff's claim is based on the following legal grounds:

(a) Breach of contract of sale between the Plaintiff and the Defendant;

(b) Dishonour of cheques issued by the Defendant in payment for goods supplied by the Plaintiff;

(c) Unjust enrichment of the Defendant at the expense of the Plaintiff;

(d) The provisions of the Sale of Goods Act, 1930, particularly Sections 49 and 55, which provide for recovery of the price of goods sold and delivered;

(e) The provisions of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, particularly Section 138, which provides for liability arising from dishonour of cheques;

(f) General principles of contract law and law of torts as applicable under the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

---

5. RELIEF SOUGHT

The Plaintiff seeks the following reliefs from this Hon'ble Court:

(1) A decree for recovery of INR 15,00,000 (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs only) from the Defendant, being the value of goods supplied by the Plaintiff to the Defendant;

(2) Interest at the rate of 12% per annum on the said amount of INR 15,00,000 from the date of cause of action (10th November, 2025) till the date of decree and thereafter at the rate prescribed by law till realization;

(3) Costs of this suit;

(4) Such further and other relief as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the circumstances of the case.

---

6. VALUATION AND COURT FEE

6.1 The value of the subject-matter of this suit is INR 15,00,000 (Rupees Fifteen Lakhs only).

6.2 The court fee payable on this plaint is calculated as per the Court Fees Act, 1870, and the applicable court fee schedule for the Commercial Division of the High Court. The court fee has been computed on the basis of the pecuniary value of the relief sought, being INR 15,00,000, and the requisite court fee of INR _________________ (amount as per applicable schedule) has been paid / is being paid herewith.

---

7. VERIFICATION

I, _________________ (name), son/daughter of _________________, aged _________________ years, resident of _________________, do hereby solemnly affirm and declare that:

(1) I am the _________________ (designation) of the Plaintiff company and am duly authorized to verify this plaint on behalf of the Plaintiff.

(2) The facts stated in paragraphs 3.1 to 3.8 of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief, and I believe them to be true.

(3) I have not suppressed any material fact or document.

(4) The relief sought is just and proper.

Verified at _________________ (place) on this _________________ (date).

                                                    _____________________
                                                    (Signature of Deponent)
                                                    (Name and Designation)
                                                    (Seal of Company, if applicable)

---

Advocate for the Plaintiff

Date: _________________

IN THE DISTRICT COURT OF PUNE

CIVIL SUIT NO. ________________

MADHAV SINGH
(Hereinafter called "the Plaintiff")

VERSUS

RAMESH BUILDERS

PLAINT

JURISDICTION

1. This Court has territorial jurisdiction to entertain this suit as the cause of action has arisen within the jurisdiction of this Court. The subject matter of the dispute relates to immovable property situated in Pune District, and the defendant is also resident/carrying on business within this jurisdiction. This Court therefore has jurisdiction under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

2. The pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court is satisfied as the suit valuation is Rs. 2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lakhs only), which falls within the pecuniary jurisdiction of the District Court.

3. The subject-matter jurisdiction of this Court is competent to entertain disputes relating to immovable property, easement rights, and injunctive relief.

CAUSE OF ACTION

4. The cause of action for this suit arose on 20th January, 2026, at Pune, when the plaintiff came to know of the obstruction caused by the defendant to his established right of way through a common passage adjoining the disputed wall. The defendant, without any legal authority or justification, constructed a wall on 15th January, 2026, thereby blocking the passage which the plaintiff has been using continuously, peacefully, and without interruption for over 25 years.

FACTS

5. The plaintiff is the owner of a property adjoining a common passage in Pune. For more than 25 years, the plaintiff has been using this common passage as a right of way to access his property and for general passage and re-passage.

6. The defendant, Ramesh Builders, is a builder/developer who owns the adjoining property and has recently undertaken construction activities in the area.

7. On or about 15th January, 2026, the defendant, without any legal justification or consent from the plaintiff, constructed a brick wall across the common passage, thereby completely obstructing the plaintiff's established right of way.

8. The plaintiff's right to use the common passage has been established through long, continuous, peaceful, and uninterrupted use for a period exceeding 25 years, thereby creating an easement by prescription under the Indian Easements Act, 1882.

9. The obstruction caused by the defendant's wall has rendered the plaintiff's property inaccessible and has caused immense hardship to the plaintiff in accessing his own property and exercising his established rights.

10. The plaintiff has made repeated requests to the defendant to remove the wall and restore the passage, but the defendant has refused to do so, acting in a high-handed and arbitrary manner.

11. The plaintiff has suffered loss and damage due to the defendant's unlawful obstruction and continues to suffer such loss and damage on a daily basis.

LEGAL GROUNDS

12. The plaintiff's right to use the common passage is protected under the Indian Easements Act, 1882, particularly Section 4 (definition of easement) and Section 15 (easement by prescription). The plaintiff has satisfied all conditions for acquiring an easement by prescription, namely: (i) the use has been continuous for over 25 years; (ii) the use has been peaceful and without force; (iii) the use has been without permission or license; and (iv) the use has been as of right.

13. The defendant's act of constructing the wall and obstructing the plaintiff's right of way constitutes a tort of nuisance and an infringement of the plaintiff's legal rights. This is actionable under the law of torts and the Indian Penal Code, 1860.

14. The plaintiff is entitled to seek injunctive relief under Section 37 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, to restrain the defendant from further obstructing his right of way and to compel the removal of the unlawful obstruction.

15. The defendant's conduct is also in violation of the plaintiff's rights under Article 19(1)(f) of the Constitution of India, which guarantees the right to property.

PRAYER FOR RELIEF

The plaintiff therefore prays for the following reliefs:

(1) A decree for a permanent injunction restraining the defendant, his agents, servants, and all persons acting in concert with him or on his behalf, from obstructing, interfering with, or in any manner preventing the plaintiff from exercising his established right of way through the common passage adjoining the disputed wall;

(2) A decree for a mandatory injunction directing the defendant to remove the wall constructed on or about 15th January, 2026, and to restore the common passage to its original condition within a period of thirty (30) days from the date of the decree;

(3) A decree for costs of this suit to be paid by the defendant to the plaintiff.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

16. The suit is valued at Rs. 2,00,000/- (Rupees Two Lakhs only) for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction. The valuation is computed on the basis of the loss and damage suffered by the plaintiff due to the obstruction of his right of way and the diminution in the value and utility of his property. The court fee has been paid in accordance with the Court Fees Act, 1870, as applicable to suits for injunctive relief and damages.

VERIFICATION

I, Madhav Singh, the above-named plaintiff, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief, and that I have not suppressed any material fact. I further verify that I have not filed any other suit in any court relating to the same cause of action.

Verified at Pune on this __________ day of __________ 2026.

(Signature of Plaintiff)
Madhav Singh

PETITION FOR DISSOLUTION OF MARRIAGE

IN THE FAMILY COURT OF CHANDIGARH

Neha Kapoor
(Petitioner)

v.

Arjun Kapoor
(Respondent)

---

1. JURISDICTION

The petitioner, Neha Kapoor, is a resident of Chandigarh and has resided therein for the last five years. The respondent, Arjun Kapoor, was last known to reside at [address to be filled], Chandigarh. This Court has territorial jurisdiction to entertain this petition under Section 19 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 (hereinafter referred to as "the Act"), as the petitioner is resident within the jurisdiction of this Court and the marriage was solemnised within the territorial limits of this Court.

2. MARRIAGE

The petitioner and respondent were united in matrimony on 14th February, 2018, at Chandigarh, in accordance with Hindu rites and ceremonies. The marriage was duly solemnised and registered in the prescribed manner. The marriage was valid and subsisting at the time of the filing of this petition.

3. COHABITATION AND CHILDREN

The petitioner and respondent cohabited as husband and wife from the date of marriage until 1st June, 2023, when the respondent abandoned the petitioner. One child, namely Aarav, aged 5 years, was born of the marriage. The said child is presently in the custody and care of the petitioner.

4. GROUNDS FOR DISSOLUTION

4.1 CRUELTY

The respondent has subjected the petitioner to cruelty within the meaning of Section 13(1)(a) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. The respondent has consistently engaged in acts of physical and mental cruelty towards the petitioner, including [specific instances of cruelty to be detailed: verbal abuse, threats, physical violence, humiliation, etc.]. These acts have caused grave injury to the mental health and well-being of the petitioner and have rendered it impossible for the petitioner to live with the respondent. The cruelty is of such a nature as to cause reasonable apprehension in the mind of the petitioner that it would be harmful or injurious to live with the respondent.

4.2 DESERTION

The respondent has deserted the petitioner within the meaning of Section 13(1)(b) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955. The respondent, without any reasonable cause and without the consent or connivance of the petitioner, abandoned the matrimonial home on 1st June, 2023, and has remained absent from the petitioner's company for a continuous period of more than two years. The respondent has shown no intention of resuming cohabitation with the petitioner. The desertion is willful and continues to persist.

5. MAINTENANCE AND CUSTODY

The petitioner seeks an order for monthly maintenance for herself and the child, Aarav, to be paid by the respondent. The petitioner further seeks confirmation of her custody of the child, Aarav, aged 5 years, and an order for the respondent to pay maintenance for the child at such amount as this Court deems fit and proper. The petitioner also seeks interim relief, including interim maintenance and interim custody, pending the final disposal of this petition.

6. PRAYER

In view of the foregoing, the petitioner humbly prays that this Court be pleased to:

(a) Dissolve the marriage solemnised between the petitioner and the respondent on 14th February, 2018;

(b) Award custody of the minor child, Aarav, aged 5 years, to the petitioner;

(c) Direct the respondent to pay monthly maintenance to the petitioner at such amount as this Court deems fit and proper;

(d) Direct the respondent to pay monthly maintenance for the minor child, Aarav, at such amount as this Court deems fit and proper;

(e) Grant interim maintenance and interim custody pending the final disposal of this petition;

(f) Pass such further and other orders as this Court may deem fit and proper in the circumstances of the case; and

(g) Award costs of this petition to the petitioner.

---

Dated: [Date]

[Petitioner's Signature]
Neha Kapoor
Petitioner

[Advocate's Name and Signature]
Advocate for the Petitioner

RE: DEMAND FOR REMOVAL OF UNAUTHORISED PARTITION ERECTED ON COMMON LANDING — FLAT 12A, SUNSHINE APARTMENTS, ANDHERI WEST, MUMBAI

Dear Sir/Madam,

This notice is issued to you in connection with the unauthorised partition wall that has been erected on the common landing area adjoining Flat 12A, Sunshine Apartments, Andheri West, Mumbai, without the consent, permission, or authority of the rightful owner.

1. PROPERTY DETAILS

The property in question is:
Flat 12A, Sunshine Apartments, Andheri West, Mumbai
[Insert Survey Number / Plot Number if available]
[Insert Area in sq. ft. / sq. m.]

2. SENDER'S TITLE AND OWNERSHIP

The undersigned, Ms. Priya Desai, is the lawful and registered owner of the aforementioned property. Title to the said property is derived through a Sale Deed dated [Insert Date of Sale Deed], registered under the Registration Act, 1908 at [Insert Name of Sub-Registrar Office], Registration Number [Insert Registration Number], Volume [Insert Volume Number], Page [Insert Page Number]. A certified copy of the said Sale Deed is enclosed herewith for your reference.

The said Sale Deed establies the complete and absolute transfer of all rights, title, and interest in the property to the undersigned in accordance with Section 5 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882.

3. NATURE OF ENCROACHMENT AND TRESPASS

It has come to the notice of the undersigned that you have, without any lawful authority, erected an unauthorised partition wall on the common landing area adjoining Flat 12A. This partition has been constructed on the common property, which forms an integral part of the building's shared infrastructure and is not within your exclusive domain.

The said unauthorised partition was erected on or about [Insert Approximate Date of Encroachment], thereby obstructing the free passage and use of the common landing by the rightful owner and other residents of the building. This action constitutes a clear trespass upon the common property and an unlawful encroachment that violates the rights of the property owner.

4. LEGAL POSITION

Your action of erecting the unauthorised partition on the common landing is in direct violation of:

(a) Section 5 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, which vests in the registered owner all rights to the property, including the right to exclusive possession and enjoyment of the common areas appurtenant to the property.

(b) Section 5 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, which provides for recovery of possession of immovable property by the rightful owner against any person wrongfully in possession or obstructing such possession.

(c) Section 6 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, which provides for summary recovery of possession in cases of forcible dispossession or unlawful occupation.

(d) The provisions of the Registration Act, 1908, which establish the title of the registered owner and protect against encroachment by unauthorised persons.

(e) The bye-laws and regulations of Sunshine Apartments, which prohibit unauthorised alterations or constructions on common property without the written consent of the building management and the owner.

You have no legal right, title, or interest in the common landing area, and your erection of the partition constitutes a clear violation of the property rights of the undersigned.

5. DEMAND FOR RELIEF

In view of the foregoing, the undersigned hereby demands that you:

(a) Immediately cease and desist from any further obstruction or use of the common landing area;

(b) Remove the unauthorised partition wall in its entirety within FIFTEEN (15) DAYS from the date of receipt of this notice;

(c) Restore the common landing to its original condition, free from any obstruction or encroachment;

(d) Provide written confirmation of compliance with this demand within the stipulated period.

The response to this demand must be received by the undersigned on or before [Insert Date — 15 days from notice date].

6. CONSEQUENCES OF NON-COMPLIANCE

Please be advised that failure to comply with this demand within the prescribed period of fifteen (15) days shall leave the undersigned with no option but to pursue all available legal remedies, including but not limited to:

(a) Filing a suit for recovery of possession of the common landing under Section 5 and Section 6 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, seeking a decree for removal of the unauthorised partition and restoration of the property to its original condition.

(b) Seeking an order for injunction to restrain you from further encroachment or obstruction of the common property.

(c) Claiming damages for loss, inconvenience, and diminution in the value of the property caused by the unauthorised partition.

(d) Initiating proceedings before the building management and the society to enforce compliance with the bye-laws and regulations.

(e) If the facts disclose the commission of a cognisable offence under the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (including offences relating to criminal trespass, mischief, or wrongful restraint), the undersigned shall not hesitate to lodge a criminal complaint with the appropriate law enforcement authorities.

All costs, legal fees, and expenses incurred in pursuing these remedies shall be claimed from you.

7. NOTICE

This is a final notice before initiating legal proceedings. The undersigned has made every effort to resolve this matter amicably and expects your immediate compliance.

Please acknowledge receipt of this notice and confirm your compliance within the stipulated period.

Yours faithfully,

Ms. Priya Desai
[Signature]
[Printed Name]
[Date]

ENCLOSURES:
1. Certified copy of Sale Deed (Registration Number [Insert Number])
2. Photographs of the unauthorised partition (if available)
3. Building bye-laws and regulations (if available)

---

scenarios.body:
PRIVATE CRIMINAL COMPLAINT UNDER BHARATIYA NYAYA SANHITA, 2023 s.223

CAUSE TITLE
Aarti Sharma (Complainant) v. Rajesh Kumar (Accused)

JURISDICTION PARAGRAPH
This complaint is filed before the Court of the Magistrate, Patiala House Courts, New Delhi, which has territorial jurisdiction over the area of Connaught Place, New Delhi, where the alleged offence was committed on 01 February 2026. This Court is competent to take cognisance of the offences alleged herein under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (hereinafter "BNS"), which came into force on 01 July 2024, and applies to all offences committed on or after that date.

PARTIES PARAGRAPH
1. The Complainant, Aarti Sharma, aged approximately 42 years, is a resident of Flat No. 305, Ashoka Apartments, Sector 8, Rohini, New Delhi – 110085. She is the aggrieved party who has suffered loss and damage as a result of the criminal conduct of the Accused.

2. The Accused, Rajesh Kumar, aged approximately 48 years, was last known to reside at House No. 27, Gali No. 5, Laxmi Nagar, New Delhi – 110092. The Accused is currently absconding and his present whereabouts are unknown. He is engaged in the business of real-estate investment schemes and has perpetrated the offences detailed herein.

FACTS IN CHRONOLOGICAL ORDER

3. On or about 15 January 2026, the Accused, Rajesh Kumar, approached the Complainant, Aarti Sharma, at Connaught Place, New Delhi, and verbally induced her to invest a sum of INR 10,00,000 (Ten Lakhs) in what he represented to be a legitimate and profitable real-estate development scheme. The Accused falsely represented that the scheme was registered with the appropriate authorities, that the investment was secure, and that the Complainant would receive substantial returns within a period of 18 months.

4. Relying upon the false representations and inducements made by the Accused, the Complainant, in good faith and with the intention of making a lawful investment, transferred the sum of INR 10,00,000 to the Accused on 01 February 2026 at Connaught Place, New Delhi, through a bank cheque bearing cheque number 456789, drawn on her account at ICICI Bank, New Delhi.

5. Following the transfer of funds, the Accused provided to the Complainant a document purporting to be an "Allotment Letter" dated 01 February 2026, which bore the letterhead of a fictitious entity named "Sharma Realty Developers Private Limited" and contained forged signatures and seals. The said Allotment Letter falsely stated that the Complainant had been allotted a residential property in a non-existent real-estate project located in Gurugram.

6. Upon subsequent inquiry and verification conducted by the Complainant in the month of February 2026, it was discovered that no such real-estate scheme exists, no such entity named "Sharma Realty Developers Private Limited" is registered with the Ministry of Corporate Affairs or any other regulatory authority, and the Allotment Letter is entirely forged and fabricated. The signatures and seals appearing on the Allotment Letter do not belong to any authorized signatory of any legitimate entity.

7. The Complainant made repeated attempts to contact the Accused and recover her investment, but the Accused deliberately avoided all communication and has since absconded from his known address. The Complainant has suffered a loss of INR 10,00,000 and has been caused immense mental anguish and financial hardship as a result of the criminal conduct of the Accused.

SECTIONS ALLEGEDLY COMMITTED

8. Based on the facts narrated above, the Accused, Rajesh Kumar, has committed the following offences:

   (a) Offence under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 s.318 (Cheating) — The Accused, by making false representations regarding the existence and legitimacy of the real-estate scheme and the security of the investment, dishonestly induced the Complainant to deliver the sum of INR 10,00,000, thereby committing the offence of cheating as defined in s.318 BNS. This offence is disclosed by the facts narrated in paragraphs 3, 4, and 7 above.

   (b) Offence under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 s.337 (Causing hurt by act endangering life or personal safety) — The Accused, by forging the Allotment Letter and presenting it as a genuine document, created a false sense of security and legitimacy, thereby endangering the financial and personal safety of the Complainant. The act of forgery and fraud has caused the Complainant to suffer hurt in the form of financial loss and psychological distress. This offence is disclosed by the facts narrated in paragraphs 5, 6, and 7 above.

PRIOR FIR POSITION

9. No First Information Report (FIR) has been filed with any police station in connection with this matter. The Complainant is filing this private criminal complaint directly before this Court under s.223 BNS because the police authorities have not registered an FIR despite the Complainant's attempts to lodge a complaint. The Complainant has chosen to exercise her right to file a private complaint in order to ensure that the criminal conduct of the Accused is brought to the notice of the Court and that justice is expeditiously delivered.

WITNESSES

10. The following witnesses are available to testify in support of the allegations made in this complaint:

    (i) Witness No. 1: Priya Verma, aged 38 years, resident of Flat No. 306, Ashoka Apartments, Sector 8, Rohini, New Delhi – 110085. Ms. Verma is a close friend and confidante of the Complainant and can testify regarding the Complainant's financial condition before and after the alleged fraud, the Complainant's state of mind, and the distress caused to the Complainant.

    (ii) Witness No. 2: Vikram Singh, aged 45 years, resident of House No. 12, Connaught Place, New Delhi – 110001. Mr. Singh is a business associate of the Complainant and was present at Connaught Place on 01 February 2026 when the Complainant met with the Accused. He can testify regarding the meeting between the Complainant and the Accused and the transfer of funds.

    (iii) Witness No. 3: Neha Kapoor, aged 35 years, employed as a Senior Manager at ICICI Bank, New Delhi Branch, resident of Apartment 5B, Cyber Hub, Gurugram – 122001. Ms. Kapoor can testify regarding the bank cheque bearing number 456789, the account details of the Complainant, and the transfer of funds on 01 February 2026.

PRAYER

11. In view of the facts and circumstances narrated above, the Complainant respectfully prays before this Hon'ble Court that:

    (a) This Court take cognisance of the offences alleged herein under s.227 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023;

    (b) This Court issue summons to the Accused, Rajesh Kumar, to appear before this Court and answer the charges against him;

    (c) This Court direct the police authorities to apprehend the Accused, who is currently absconding, and produce him before this Court;

    (d) This Court proceed with the trial in accordance with the provisions of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023;

    (e) This Court, upon conviction of the Accused, impose appropriate punishment as provided under law and direct the Accused to pay compensation to the Complainant for the loss suffered; and

    (f) This Court grant such other and further relief as it may deem fit and proper in the interests of justice.

Dated: [Date of Filing]

[Signature of Complainant or Authorized Representative]
Aarti Sharma
Complainant

[Signature of Advocate, if any]
[Name and Bar Council Registration Number]
